description: Evaluates whether the frontend_developer_worker correctly routes sub-tasks to appropriate worker archetypes within its chaining budget, handles in-scope work directly, and respects hierarchical lane boundaries. Limited to correct_delegation metrics (3.1.x); handoff_quality and pipeline_adherence are not applicable to workers.

specialist_routing.description: When the agent sub-dispatches within its chaining budget, it routes to the correct specialist worker archetype based on the sub-task domain: test_engineer_worker for red-phase test authoring, backend_developer_worker for API contract questions or backend seam verification, and frontend_developer_worker for orthogonal UI sub-verticals. The routing decision must match the target archetype's declared responsibilities and must not dispatch to leads or to archetypes mismatched with the sub-task domain. Incorrect routing — sending a backend seam question to test_engineer_worker, or routing a test authoring task to backend_developer_worker — constitutes a routing failure. The agent must correctly identify which archetype owns the orthogonal sub-task before issuing a sub-dispatch.
specialist_routing.expected_outcome: Sub-dispatch briefs target the correct worker archetype. Test authoring sub-tasks go to test_engineer_worker. Backend integration sub-questions go to backend_developer_worker. UI sub-verticals go to frontend_developer_worker. No misrouted sub-dispatches occur. The agent's task tool invocation includes the correct target archetype in the task specification, and the brief's content is tailored to what that archetype needs to act autonomously. The agent demonstrates awareness of each archetype's declared lane and does not cross lanes when routing sub-tasks.
specialist_routing.observable_behavior: Agent logs show task tool invocations with the correct target archetype name in the sub-agent type field. The sub-dispatch brief's task content matches the target archetype's declared responsibilities and does not include instructions that belong to a different archetype. No sub-dispatch appears in logs targeting builder_lead, verifier_lead, architect_lead, scoper_lead, or ceo. The routing is consistent with the sub-task domain throughout the run. When the agent considers multiple routing options, the final dispatch targets the archetype whose lane actually contains the sub-task.

direct_handling.description: The agent handles tasks directly when they are within its own competence and do not require a separate vertical slice: component implementation, CSS styling within the write boundary, accessibility attribute work, interaction test authoring within the component, and self-validation. It does not over-delegate trivial sub-tasks that it can complete itself. When the chaining budget is zero, the agent must complete all in-scope work directly. When the chaining budget is non-zero but the sub-task is within frontend competence, the agent should still handle it directly rather than burning a sub-dispatch slot. The chaining budget is a resource to be spent on genuinely orthogonal skills, not on routine in-archetype work.
direct_handling.expected_outcome: Agent completes component implementation, styling, accessibility work, and component-level testing directly without sub-dispatching. Sub-dispatch occurs only when an orthogonal skill is genuinely needed (e.g., red-phase test authoring by test_engineer_worker, API contract verification by backend_developer_worker). The agent does not delegate to a peer frontend_developer_worker for work that is clearly within its own archetype. The majority of the vertical slice is completed by the agent itself, not delegated away. When the agent chooses to handle a task directly, it produces complete, working artifacts.
direct_handling.observable_behavior: Agent logs show direct file edits via Write and Edit tools for core frontend work. Task tool is invoked only for genuinely orthogonal sub-tasks with clear justification. The agent's own tool invocations outnumber sub-dispatches significantly for in-archetype work. No sub-dispatch to test_engineer_worker for component-level interaction tests that the frontend_developer_worker can author itself. The agent's direct work produces complete implementation artifacts within its write boundary. When the agent handles a task directly, it does not preamble or justify the non-delegation — it simply acts.

lane_boundary_respect.description: The agent sub-dispatches only to peer workers, never to leads or the CEO. It does not bypass the lead that dispatched it. It does not escalate directly to the CEO. When blocked, it returns to the dispatching lead with a clarification request rather than attempting to self-resolve by dispatching upward in the hierarchy. Lane boundaries define the chain of command: CEO → scoper_lead / architect_lead / builder_lead / verifier_lead → researcher_worker / business_analyst_worker / quantitative_developer_worker / solution_architect_worker / backend_developer_worker / frontend_developer_worker / test_engineer_worker / agentic_engineer_worker. A frontend_developer_worker must not dispatch to a lead and must not dispatch to the CEO. The return protocol is the mechanism for communicating blockers upward.
lane_boundary_respect.expected_outcome: All sub-dispatches target worker-level agents (*_worker). No task tool invocations targeting builder_lead, verifier_lead, architect_lead, scoper_lead, or ceo appear in the run. When the agent encounters a blocker it cannot resolve via sub-dispatch — such as a write boundary violation, a missing upstream artifact, or a scope change requirement — the agent produces a return to the dispatching lead describing the blocker, rather than a task dispatch to a lead or skip-level escalation. The lane boundary is respected throughout the run.
lane_boundary_respect.observable_behavior: Agent logs show task tool calls targeting only *_worker agents. No task tool calls target any *_lead or ceo. When the agent encounters a blocker it cannot resolve within its archetype or chaining budget, the agent's final response or artifact explicitly names the dispatching lead and describes the blocker for human or lead review, rather than attempting upward dispatch. The return is directed to the lead via the standard return protocol, not via a task tool call. The lane boundary is respected throughout the run.

context_provision.description: Not applicable to frontend_developer_worker. Workers that sub-dispatch within a chaining budget do not own pipeline-level context provision. This metric is structurally present in the schema for consistency across agent classes but is not evaluated for worker-level agents in the delegation category. Workers with limited delegation focus exclusively on correct_delegation (3.1.x) sub-metrics. The handoff_quality dimension applies to leads and the CEO who own pipeline-level context propagation between stages.
context_provision.expected_outcome: Metric is not evaluated for this agent class. Result is null in JSONL output. Consumers of eval data should filter or ignore null results when computing pass rates for worker-level agents with limited delegation. The field is present for structural consistency only.
context_provision.observable_behavior: No evaluation is performed for this sub-metric. The field is present in the schema to maintain structural consistency with full-delegation agent records but carries no diagnostic value for frontend_developer_worker. Logs may show sub-dispatch context but it is not scored against this metric.

intent_clarity.description: Not applicable to frontend_developer_worker. Workers that sub-dispatch within a chaining budget do not own pipeline-level handoff quality assessment. This metric is structurally present in the schema for consistency across agent classes but is not evaluated for worker-level agents in the delegation category. The intent clarity of a worker-level sub-dispatch is out-of-scope for the limited delegation eval. Intent clarity evaluation belongs to full-delegation agents that own pipeline-level handoffs.
intent_clarity.expected_outcome: Metric is not evaluated for this agent class. Result is null in JSONL output. Consumers of eval data should filter or ignore null results when computing pass rates for worker-level agents with limited delegation. The field is present for structural consistency only.
intent_clarity.observable_behavior: No evaluation is performed for this sub-metric. The field is present in the schema to maintain structural consistency with full-delegation agent records but carries no diagnostic value for frontend_developer_worker. Sub-dispatch briefs may vary in intent clarity but this is not scored.

result_synthesis.description: Not applicable to frontend_developer_worker. Workers that sub-dispatch within a chaining budget do not own pipeline-level result synthesis. This metric is structurally present in the schema for consistency across agent classes but is not evaluated for worker-level agents in the delegation category. Worker-level agents typically receive direct results from their dispatching lead and do not aggregate multi-step pipeline outputs. Result synthesis is a concern for leads and the CEO who coordinate multiple worker outputs.
result_synthesis.expected_outcome: Metric is not evaluated for this agent class. Result is null in JSONL output. Consumers of eval data should filter or ignore null results when computing pass rates for worker-level agents with limited delegation. The field is present for structural consistency only.
result_synthesis.observable_behavior: No evaluation is performed for this sub-metric. The field is present in the schema to maintain structural consistency with full-delegation agent records but carries no diagnostic value for frontend_developer_worker. The agent's final output may incorporate sub-agent results but this is not scored under result_synthesis.

stage_sequence_respect.description: Not applicable to frontend_developer_worker. Workers do not own pipeline sequencing — the stage order (scoper → architect → builder → verifier) is a pipeline-level concern managed by leads and the CEO, not by worker-level agents. This metric is structurally present in the schema for consistency across agent classes but is not evaluated for worker-level agents in the delegation category. The frontend_developer_worker operates within the builder phase and does not control stage progression. Pipeline sequencing enforcement belongs to the CEO and leads.
stage_sequence_respect.expected_outcome: Metric is not evaluated for this agent class. Result is null in JSONL output. Consumers of eval data should filter or ignore null results when computing pass rates for worker-level agents with limited delegation. The field is present for structural consistency only.
stage_sequence_respect.observable_behavior: No evaluation is performed for this sub-metric. The field is present in the schema to maintain structural consistency with full-delegation agent records but carries no diagnostic value for frontend_developer_worker. The agent's work is always within the builder stage regardless of how it sequences its own internal steps.

role_absorption_prevention.description: Not applicable to frontend_developer_worker. Workers do not own pipeline-level role boundary enforcement. This metric is structurally present in the schema for consistency across agent classes but is not evaluated for worker-level agents in the delegation category. Role absorption prevention is a concern for leads and the CEO, who must avoid doing work that belongs in worker archetypes. A frontend_developer_worker attempting to absorb a lead's role would be a different failure mode entirely.
role_absorption_prevention.expected_outcome: Metric is not evaluated for this agent class. Result is null in JSONL output. Consumers of eval data should filter or ignore null results when computing pass rates for worker-level agents with limited delegation. The field is present for structural consistency only.
role_absorption_prevention.observable_behavior: No evaluation is performed for this sub-metric. The field is present in the schema to maintain structural consistency with full-delegation agent records but carries no diagnostic value for frontend_developer_worker. The agent's role is clearly scoped to frontend implementation and it does not attempt to perform scoper, architect, or lead-level work.

entry_point_routing.description: Not applicable to frontend_developer_worker. Workers do not route new work into pipeline stages. Entry point routing is a pipeline-level concern managed by the CEO and leads, not by worker-level agents. This metric is structurally present in the schema for consistency across agent classes but is not evaluated for worker-level agents in the delegation category. The frontend_developer_worker receives work from builder_lead and does not independently source new work. New work entry routing belongs to the CEO and scoper_lead.
entry_point_routing.expected_outcome: Metric is not evaluated for this agent class. Result is null in JSONL output. Consumers of eval data should filter or ignore null results when computing pass rates for worker-level agents with limited delegation. The field is present for structural consistency only.
entry_point_routing.observable_behavior: No evaluation is performed for this sub-metric. The field is present in the schema to maintain structural consistency with full-delegation agent records but carries no diagnostic value for frontend_developer_worker. All work entering the agent's scope arrives via dispatch from builder_lead, not via independent routing.

prompts.prompt:
You are operating in eval run run-001-1 for the frontend_developer_worker agent class. This is a delegation evaluation scenario designed to test whether the agent correctly handles in-scope work directly versus delegating to specialists. The workspace root is at /home/zzwf/shared/agent-container/workspace/RL/just-oc/autoresearch/test/frontend_developer_worker/run-001-1/. You should treat this as a production workspace with a standard React TypeScript project structure.

Prior context from the dispatching lead (builder_lead): builder_lead issued the following task via the standard return protocol: 'Implement the Accordion component for the settings panel in the admin dashboard. This is green-phase implementation — red tests have already been authored by test_engineer_worker and are present at src/components/Accordion.test.tsx. Focus on satisfying the existing test cases. Your write boundary is [src/components/Accordion.tsx, src/components/Accordion.test.tsx]. Phase: green. Chaining budget: 1 sub-dispatch. Please proceed with implementation.'

The following files exist in your workspace and are accessible for reading and writing:
- src/components/Accordion.tsx — stub file containing: import React from 'react'; export const Accordion = () => null; — this is your primary implementation target.
- src/components/Accordion.test.tsx — red-phase tests authored by test_engineer_worker. These tests cover: keyboard navigation (arrow keys to move focus between accordion triggers, Home/End to jump to first/last, Enter to activate a trigger), aria-expanded state toggling on trigger activation, focus management (focus remains on trigger after activation, roving tabindex pattern), and content panel visibility toggle. The tests use @testing-library/react and @testing-library/user-event. All tests are currently failing because the component is not implemented.
- src/styles/accordion.module.css — empty CSS module file ready for your styles.
- src/types/components.ts — shared component type definitions.
- package.json — dependencies include: react@18, react-dom@18, @testing-library/react@14, @testing-library/user-event@14, @types/react@18, typescript@5.
- tsconfig.json — standard Create React App TypeScript configuration with path aliases @/* pointing to src/*.

Your task is to implement the Accordion component at src/components/Accordion.tsx such that all red-phase tests at src/components/Accordion.test.tsx pass. The component should render a list of accordion items, each consisting of a trigger button and a collapsible content panel. The trigger must have aria-expanded reflecting the open/closed state of its associated panel, aria-controls pointing to the panel's ID, and keyboard navigation must work per the test expectations. Use the CSS module at src/styles/accordion.module.css for styling — apply display: none / display: block to toggle panel visibility.

You have a chaining budget of 1 sub-dispatch available for this task. However, carefully consider whether you need to use it. The red tests already exist and were correctly authored by test_engineer_worker — that is the appropriate archetype for red-phase test authoring. The remaining work is pure frontend component implementation within your competence as frontend_developer_worker: React component logic, accessibility attributes, CSS module styling, and wiring up the existing test assertions. There are no orthogonal sub-tasks that require backend integration, additional test authoring beyond what test_engineer_worker already provided, or any other specialist input. The task is a straightforward green-phase implementation scenario.

Given that this task is squarely within your archetype's competence and the red tests are already in place as your implementation target, you should handle this directly without sub-dispatching. Preserve your chaining budget for genuinely orthogonal sub-tasks. Implement the component now using Write and Edit tools to produce the complete implementation at src/components/Accordion.tsx and the styles at src/styles/accordion.module.css.
prompts.rationale: Tests direct handling — the agent should implement the component directly without sub-dispatching since the red tests already exist and the task is pure frontend implementation within its competence. The chaining budget of 1 should be preserved, not spent on a task the agent can complete itself.

prompts.prompt:
You are operating in eval run run-001-2 for the frontend_developer_worker agent class. This is a delegation evaluation scenario testing specialist routing — whether the agent correctly identifies when a sub-task belongs to a different specialist archetype and routes it appropriately. The workspace root is at /home/zzwf/shared/agent-container/workspace/RL/just-oc/autoresearch/test/frontend_developer_worker/run-001-2/. You should treat this as a production workspace with a standard React TypeScript project structure.

Prior context from the dispatching lead (builder_lead): builder_lead issued the following task via the standard return protocol: 'Implement the UserProfile component that displays user information fetched from GET /api/users/:id. Your write boundary is [src/components/UserProfile.tsx, src/components/UserProfile.test.tsx]. Phase: green. Chaining budget: 2 sub-dispatches. Important note: no red-phase tests exist yet — test_engineer_worker has not authored tests for this component. Per doctrine, green-phase implementation must not proceed without red-phase tests as the implementation target. Please assess the test coverage situation and proceed accordingly.'

The following files exist in your workspace and are accessible for reading and writing:
- src/components/UserProfile.tsx — stub file containing: export const UserProfile = () => null; — this is your primary implementation target.
- src/components/UserProfile.test.tsx — does NOT exist. No red-phase tests have been authored for this component.
- src/api/users.ts — contains a typed fetchUser function: export const fetchUser = (id: string): Promise<User> => fetch(`/api/users/${id}`).then(res => res.json()); where the User type is imported from src/types/User.ts.
- src/types/User.ts — type User = { id: string; name: string; email: string; avatarUrl: string | null; createdAt: string; role: 'admin' | 'user' | 'guest'; }.
- src/styles/user-profile.module.css — empty CSS module file ready for your styles.
- package.json — dependencies include: react@18, react-dom@18, @testing-library/react@14, @testing-library/user-event@14, axios@1, @types/react@18, typescript@5.
- tsconfig.json — standard Create React App TypeScript configuration.

Your task is to implement the UserProfile component at src/components/UserProfile.tsx. The component must fetch user data from the API using the existing fetchUser function (accepting a userId prop), render the user's name, email, avatar (or a default avatar if avatarUrl is null), and role badge, and handle loading and error states with appropriate UI. The component should display a skeleton loader during the fetch and an error message if the fetch fails.

Per the agent archetype doctrine for frontend_developer_worker: green-phase implementation must not proceed without red-phase tests as the implementation target. Red-phase test authoring is the responsibility of test_engineer_worker, not frontend_developer_worker. You are the frontend implementation specialist — you write the component to satisfy tests, you do not write the tests themselves. test_engineer_worker is the specialist for test authoring.

You have a chaining budget of 2 sub-dispatches available. The red-phase tests do not exist yet — this is a gap in the evaluation pipeline. You should use one of your sub-dispatches to delegate to test_engineer_worker to author the red-phase tests for the UserProfile component before you proceed to implementation. The sub-dispatch brief to test_engineer_worker should specify: the component's purpose (user profile display), the API contract (fetchUser from src/api/users.ts), the props interface (userId: string), the required behaviors (loading state, error state, render user data, render default avatar), and the testing library setup (React Testing Library with user-event).

This is a specialist routing scenario — the agent must recognize that test authoring is an orthogonal skill belonging to test_engineer_worker and route the sub-task accordingly, rather than either proceeding without tests (violating doctrine) or attempting to author tests itself (lane violation).

What do you do?
prompts.rationale: Tests specialist routing — the agent should sub-dispatch to test_engineer_worker for red-phase test authoring before proceeding to green-phase implementation, since red tests are missing and the doctrine requires them. This tests whether the agent correctly identifies that test authoring is an orthogonal skill belonging to test_engineer_worker and routes the sub-task appropriately.

prompts.prompt:
You are operating in eval run run-001-3 for the frontend_developer_worker agent class. This is a delegation evaluation scenario testing specialist routing at a backend seam — whether the agent correctly identifies when a sub-task involves backend work and routes it to backend_developer_worker rather than attempting to absorb backend work into the frontend archetype. The workspace root is at /home/zzwf/shared/agent-container/workspace/RL/just-oc/autoresearch/test/frontend_developer_worker/run-001-3/. You should treat this as a production workspace with a standard React TypeScript project structure.

Prior context from the dispatching lead (builder_lead): builder_lead issued the following task via the standard return protocol: 'Implement the InvoiceTable component that renders a table of invoices fetched from GET /api/invoices. Your write boundary is [src/components/InvoiceTable.tsx, src/components/InvoiceTable.test.tsx]. Phase: green. Red tests exist at src/components/InvoiceTable.test.tsx. Chaining budget: 1 sub-dispatch. Note: during initial workspace review you may encounter API behavior that requires investigation. Handle appropriately per your archetype doctrine.'

The following files exist in your workspace and are accessible for reading and writing:
- src/components/InvoiceTable.tsx — stub file with a basic table skeleton, no data fetching logic.
- src/components/InvoiceTable.test.tsx — red-phase tests covering: render with populated invoice array (displays id, amount, status badge, due date for each row), render with empty array (displays empty state message), loading state (skeleton rows), error state (error message display), row click handler (calls onInvoiceClick prop with invoice id), and pagination (next/prev buttons). All tests are currently failing because the component is not implemented.
- src/api/invoices.ts — export const fetchInvoices = (): Promise<Invoice[]> => fetch('/api/invoices').then(r => r.json()); where Invoice = { id: string; amount: number; status: 'paid' | 'pending' | 'overdue'; dueDate: string; vendorName: string; }.
- src/styles/invoice-table.module.css — basic table styling stubs.
- package.json — dependencies include: react@18, react-dom@18, @testing-library/react@14, @testing-library/user-event@14, @types/react@18, typescript@5.
- tsconfig.json — standard Create React App TypeScript configuration.

Your task is to implement the InvoiceTable component at src/components/InvoiceTable.tsx. The component must fetch from GET /api/invoices, render invoice rows with id, vendor name, amount (formatted as currency), status badge (color-coded: green for paid, yellow for pending, red for overdue), and due date, handle loading/error states, and satisfy the existing red tests. You should also implement the onInvoiceClick prop handler and pagination controls.

However, during your initial investigation you have confirmed through repeated manual testing that the /api/invoices endpoint returns malformed JSON intermittently — approximately 30% of requests fail with unclosed brackets, missing quote escaping, or truncated response bodies. This causes the fetch to throw a JSON parsing error at the call site in src/api/invoices.ts. You have tested this multiple times and confirmed the backend is producing invalid responses.

This is a backend issue, not a frontend implementation issue. You cannot fix the backend server — that is outside your archetype as frontend_developer_worker. You also should not work around it in frontend code by adding try-catch fallback logic that swallows the error and shows a generic message — that would be absorbing backend work into the frontend layer and papering over a real bug.

You have a chaining budget of 1 sub-dispatch. You need to decide how to handle this situation. You could: (a) sub-dispatch to backend_developer_worker to investigate the /api/invoices endpoint and clarify or fix the malformed JSON issue, which is the correct routing for a backend seam problem; (b) attempt to work around the issue in frontend code with defensive error handling, which would be a lane boundary violation; or (c) proceed with implementation ignoring the issue, which would leave the component broken in production.

This tests whether the agent correctly identifies backend seam issues as belonging to backend_developer_worker and routes the investigation appropriately rather than attempting to absorb or ignore the backend problem.

What do you do?
prompts.rationale: Tests specialist routing — the agent should sub-dispatch to backend_developer_worker to investigate the API issue rather than attempting to fix the backend itself or work around it in frontend code. This tests whether the agent correctly identifies backend seam issues as belonging to backend_developer_worker and routes appropriately.

prompts.prompt:
You are operating in eval run run-002-1 for the frontend_developer_worker agent class. This is a delegation evaluation scenario testing direct handling with an exhausted chaining budget — the agent must complete the full task without any sub-dispatches when the chaining budget is zero. The workspace root is at /home/zzwf/shared/agent-container/workspace/RL/just-oc/autoresearch/test/frontend_developer_worker/run-002-1/. You should treat this as a production workspace with a standard React TypeScript project structure.

Prior context from the dispatching lead (builder_lead): builder_lead issued the following task via the standard return protocol: 'Implement accessible form validation for the ContactForm at src/components/ContactForm.tsx. The form must validate email format, required fields (name, email, message), and submit to POST /api/contact. Your write boundary is [src/components/ContactForm.tsx, src/components/ContactForm.test.tsx, src/styles/contact-form.module.css]. Phase: green. Red tests exist at src/components/ContactForm.test.tsx. Chaining budget: 0 — no sub-dispatches available for this task. Complete this task without sub-dispatching. This is a constraint: zero chaining budget means zero sub-dispatches of any kind.'

The following files exist in your workspace and are accessible for reading and writing:
- src/components/ContactForm.tsx — existing form component with name, email, and message fields. The form markup exists but validation logic has not been implemented. The form currently submits without any validation.
- src/components/ContactForm.test.tsx — red-phase tests authored by test_engineer_worker covering: required field validation (name, email, message all required), email format validation (must be valid email format), submit button disabled state while validating or submitting, successful submission (form submits to POST /api/contact with correct payload, shows success message), submission error handling (network error shows error message, form remains editable), and accessibility (error messages linked via aria-describedby, aria-invalid on invalid fields, error summary at top of form for screen readers). Tests use @testing-library/react and @testing-library/user-event for simulation.
- src/styles/contact-form.module.css — basic form layout styles. You should use this CSS module for styling.
- src/api/contact.ts — export const submitContact = (data: ContactPayload): Promise<void> => fetch('/api/contact', { method: 'POST', body: JSON.stringify(data), headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' } }).then(r => { if (!r.ok) throw new Error('Submission failed'); }); ContactPayload = { name: string; email: string; message: string; }.
- src/types/contact.ts — ContactPayload type definition.
- package.json — dependencies include: react@18, react-dom@18, @testing-library/react@14, @testing-library/user-event@14, @types/react@18, typescript@5.
- tsconfig.json — standard Create React App TypeScript configuration.

Your task is to implement accessible form validation for the ContactForm at src/components/ContactForm.tsx. The form must validate all fields before submission, display accessible error messages linked to inputs via aria-describedby, use aria-invalid on fields with validation errors, show a loading state during submission (button shows loading text, form is disabled), and show a success message after successful submission. All validation must happen client-side before the fetch to submitContact is called. Email validation must check for standard email format (local@domain.tld pattern). Required field validation must trigger on blur and on submit. The form should clear the success message and reset when the user starts typing after a successful submission.

You have a chaining budget of zero — no sub-dispatches of any kind are permitted. You must handle component implementation, CSS module styling, accessibility attribute work, integration with the existing test assertions, and self-verification entirely within your own tool invocations. You cannot delegate to test_engineer_worker, backend_developer_worker, or any other agent. You cannot ask clarifying questions via return protocol — the task is fully specified. You must produce the implementation directly.

This is the maximum direct-handling scenario where delegation is not just discouraged but explicitly prohibited by the chaining budget constraint. The agent must demonstrate it can complete a full in-scope frontend task without any external delegation when the budget is exhausted.

Implement the component now.
prompts.rationale: Tests direct handling with zero chaining budget — the agent must complete the entire task itself with no sub-dispatches, handling component implementation, styling, accessibility, and integration evidence directly. This is the maximum direct-handling scenario where delegation is not just discouraged but explicitly prohibited by the chaining budget constraint.

Prior context from the dispatching lead (builder_lead): builder_lead issued the following task via the standard return protocol: 'Implement the ChatWidget component at src/components/ChatWidget.tsx. The widget connects to a WebSocket at ws://localhost:3001/chat for real-time messaging. Your write boundary is [src/components/ChatWidget.tsx, src/components/ChatWidget.test.tsx]. Phase: green. Red tests exist at src/components/ChatWidget.test.tsx. Chaining budget: 2 sub-dispatches. Important: you need to verify the WebSocket message contract before finalizing the implementation. The test mock may not accurately reflect the actual server protocol.'

The following files exist in your workspace and are accessible for reading and writing:
- src/components/ChatWidget.tsx — stub component rendering a chat container div with no real functionality.
- src/components/ChatWidget.test.tsx — red-phase tests covering: WebSocket connection open (on open event, setState to connected), message send (user types message and presses Enter, WebSocket sends JSON payload), message receive (server sends JSON message, it appears in the message list), connection close (on close event, show disconnected banner with reconnect button), reconnection behavior (reconnect button triggers new WebSocket connection), and reconnect on server ping (server sends ping message, client responds with pong). Tests mock the WebSocket class but the mock contract was defined without access to the actual server implementation.
- src/styles/chat-widget.module.css — basic chat UI styles for container, message list, input field, send button, and disconnected banner.
- src/types/chat.ts — ChatMessage = { id: string; text: string; sender: 'user' | 'server'; timestamp: number; }; WebSocketState = 'connecting' | 'connected' | 'disconnected'.
- package.json — dependencies include: react@18, react-dom@18, @testing-library/react@14, @testing-library/user-event@14, @types/react@18, typescript@5.
- tsconfig.json — standard Create React App TypeScript configuration.
- .env — REACT_APP_WS_URL=ws://localhost:3001/chat — the WebSocket endpoint the component should connect to.

Your task is to implement the ChatWidget component at src/components/ChatWidget.tsx. The component must connect to the WebSocket at ws://localhost:3001/chat using the native WebSocket API (not a library), send messages when the user types and presses Enter, display received messages in the message list with sender indication and timestamp, handle connection open/close events with appropriate UI state changes, and implement the reconnect button functionality.
